title: North Indian Onion Pakora

ingredients.name: onion (large, sliced into thin rings or half-moons)

subs.name: thinly sliced cabbage, spinach leaves, or sliced raw banana make excellent pakoras with the same batter
a 50:50 mix of besan and rice flour produces an even crispier shell; rice flour alone makes gluten-free pakoras
a pinch of dried thyme as a mild flavour substitute, or omit entirely if unavailable

steps.text: Separate the onion slices into rings and half-moons in a large bowl. Sprinkle a generous pinch of salt over them and toss well. Let them sit for 5 minutes — the salt draws out moisture, which helps the batter cling and makes the onion soften slightly inside the finished pakora.

Add besan, rice flour, red chilli powder, turmeric, ajwain, cumin seeds, green chilli, and coriander leaves to the salted onions. Mix with your hands, squeezing the onion into the dry flour so the natural juices from the onion help bind the mixture. Add water gradually — start with 80 ml and add more only if needed — until the onions are coated in a thick, sticky batter that holds together when you press a handful. Add the baking soda if using and mix once more.

Pour oil into a deep kadai or heavy pan to a depth of 8–10 cm. Heat over medium-high flame until a small drop of batter dropped in sizzles immediately and rises to the surface. The oil should be around 175°C. Keeping the heat at medium is critical — too hot and the batter darkens before the onion cooks through.

Using your fingers or a tablespoon, drop rough clumps of the batter-coated onion into the oil, each about the size of a golf ball. Do not overcrowd — fry in two or three batches. Fry for 4–5 minutes, turning once halfway through, until each pakora is deep golden-brown and crisp all over. Lift out with a slotted spoon and drain on paper towels.

Serve immediately in a newspaper-lined bowl or on a plate. Accompany with mint-coriander chutney and tomato ketchup. Pakoras are best eaten within 10 minutes of frying. If making a second batch, keep the first batch loosely tented with foil rather than covering tightly, which traps steam and softens the crust.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: onion → thinly sliced cabbage, spinach leaves, or sliced raw banana make excellent pakoras with the same batter; besan → a 50:50 mix of besan and rice flour produces an even crispier shell; rice flour alone makes gluten-free pakoras; ajwain → a pinch of dried thyme as a mild flavour substitute, or omit entirely if unavailable
